g.data-narration: Welcome to Protection in Operating Systems. We'll explore how systems control resource access securely.
Protection controls program and user access to system resources, ensuring controlled sharing in multiprogramming environments.
g.text: Mechanism for controlling access of programs, processes, or users to system resources.
Distinct from security (external threats) - protection is INTERNAL control.

g.data-narration: Protection goals include preventing unauthorized access, enforcing policies, detecting misuse, and improving system reliability.
The Principle of Least Privilege states that programs and users should receive minimum privileges needed for their tasks.
g.text: Programs, users, and systems should be given just enough privileges to perform tasks—no more.

g.data-narration: A protection domain defines a set of objects and the types of operations that can be performed on each object.
g.text: Domain = { (object, rights-set) } where each pair defines accessible objects and allowed operations.

g.data-narration: The Access Matrix model has domains as rows, objects as columns, and entries define the rights for each domain-object pair.
Global Table stores all access triples (domain, object, rights) but becomes large and slow due to disk access requirements.
g.text: Simple ordered triples: (Domain, Object, Rights). But large and stored on disk—slow access.
(D1, F1, R/W) | (D1, F2, R) | (D2, Printer, Print) | ...

g.data-narration: ACL stores access rights with each object—columns of the access matrix. Easy to determine who can access an object.
g.text: Each object stores list of (domain, rights). Corresponds to columns. Easy revocation.
D1: R/W | D2: R | D4: R/W

g.data-narration: Capability Lists store rights with each domain—rows of the matrix. Easy delegation but difficult revocation.
g.text: Each domain stores (object, rights) pairs. Corresponds to rows. Easy delegation, hard revocation.

g.data-narration: Lock-Key uses locks on objects and keys in domains—a flexible compromise between ACL and capabilities.
g.text: Compromise: Objects have locks, domains have keys. Access granted when key matches lock.

g.data-narration: ACL is easy to revoke access but hard to delegate. Capability lists make delegation easy but revocation difficult.
Revocation removes access rights—easy with ACL, hard with capabilities. RBAC assigns users to roles for easier management.
g.text: Simplifies admin: assign roles not individual permissions

g.data-narration: Key concepts: Protection controls internal access, least privilege grants minimum rights, domains define accessible objects.
For exams: Practice drawing the Access Matrix, know implementation trade-offs, and remember protection differs from security.
g.text: 1. Practice drawing Access Matrix | 2. Know 4 implementations & trade-offs | 3. ACL vs Capability = common question
4. Remember: Protection ≠ Security (internal vs external) | 5. Least Privilege example is frequently tested
